d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Formation of the Greek Polis System
v.s: In the 8th century BC, the Greek world experienced the formation of the polis (city-state) system. Due to a mountainous geography that hindered land transport, along with numerous natural harbors and islands, Greece gradually developed independent political entities centered on a city and incorporating surrounding rural areas—the poleis. Each polis had its own laws, government, and army, with the collective rule of citizens as the core political concept. Athens, Sparta, Corinth, Thebes, and other poleis rose one after another, becoming the basic units of classical Greek civilization.

v.t: Widespread Dissemination of the Phoenician Alphabet around the Mediterranean
v.s: The 22 consonant letters created by the Phoenicians were widely adopted by Mediterranean peoples due to their simplicity and efficiency. The Greeks added vowels to form the Greek alphabet; the Aramaeans adapted the Phoenician script to develop the Aramaic alphabet, which in turn influenced Hebrew, Arabic, and several Indian writing systems. The Phoenician alphabet became the ancestor of most subsequent alphabetic scripts, greatly advancing the recording and exchange of human civilization.

v.t: Final Redaction of the Homeric Epics, the Iliad and the Odyssey
v.s: Around the 8th century BC, the ancient Greek blind poet Homer, building on a long tradition of oral epic poetry, compiled and finalized the two epics, the Iliad and the Odyssey. The Iliad recounts the wrath of Achilles and the heroic deeds during the Trojan War, while the Odyssey narrates the decade-long wanderings of Odysseus after the war and his return home. These two epics are not only the fountainhead of Western literature but also provide rich material for ancient Greek mythology, religion, society, and moral concepts.

v.t: Accession of King Xuan of Zhou, Ushering in a Revival
v.s: In 827 BC, Ji Jing ascended the throne as King Xuan of Zhou. Learning from the mistakes of his father, King Li of Zhou, whose oppressive policies had triggered a popular uprising, he appointed capable ministers such as Duke Mu of Shao, Yin Jifu, and Zhong Shanfu, reorganized the court, abolished the harsh laws of King Li's era, and restored the situation where 'all vassals again respected the Zhou court'. During his reign, King Xuan launched multiple military campaigns against the Rong and Di tribes, achieving a series of victories, known as the 'Revival under King Xuan', which temporarily delayed the decline of the Western Zhou.

v.t: Rise of Carthage as the Hegemon of the Western Mediterranean
v.s: Around 800 BC, Phoenician colonists from Tyre founded Carthage (near present-day Tunis) in North Africa. Carthage exploited its advantageous geographic position to control western Mediterranean trade, rapidly developing into a maritime empire. Carthaginians conducted a circumnavigation of Africa (c. 600 BC) and established trading posts in West Africa, exchanging gold, ivory, and slaves with local tribes. Carthage was eventually destroyed by Rome in the Punic Wars (264-146 BC), and its African territories became the Roman province of Africa.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Sparta Completes the Conquest of Laconia
v.s: After the reforms of Lycurgus, the Spartan polis strengthened its militaristic society. In the late 8th century BC, Sparta conquered Laconia through the two Messenian Wars, turning the native inhabitants into helots (state-owned serfs). Spartan citizens became professional warriors, undergoing rigorous military training and common mess halls, laying the foundation for Sparta becoming the strongest land army in the Greek world.

v.t: Assyrian Empire Suppresses Aramean Rebellion
v.s: In the late reign of King Adad-nirari III of Assyria, Aramean tribes in the western part of the empire rose in rebellion. The Assyrian general Shamshi-ilu led forces to suppress the revolt, re-establishing control over the important trade routes of northern Syria. The Assyrians implemented a deportation policy, moving some rebellious inhabitants to Mesopotamia while settling other peoples in their place to weaken local resistance.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Egypt under the 22nd Dynasty Sees Increased Fragmentation
v.s: Pharaoh Osorkon III of the 22nd Dynasty (Libyan origin) was unable to control the entire country. In Upper Egypt, the priests of Amun in Thebes became effectively independent, while in the Nile Delta, several small local kingdoms emerged. This fragmentation lasted nearly a century, providing an opportunity for the later conquest of Egypt by the Kingdom of Kush.

v.t: Phoenicians Improve Shipbuilding Technology and Develop Long-distance Trade
v.s: The Phoenicians developed stronger ship keels and adopted a two-level oar arrangement, building merchant vessels with greater capacity and speed. Using these ships, they sailed from the eastern Mediterranean as far west as beyond the Strait of Gibraltar, establishing trading posts even on the west coast of Africa. Phoenician merchants became the architects of the Mediterranean trade network, dealing in Lebanese cedar, Tyrian purple dye, glassware, and metal products.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: King Xuan of Zhou Sends Troops against the Taiyuan Rong
v.s: King Xuan of Zhou ordered Nan Zhong to lead an expedition against the Taiyuan Rong, who lived in the area around present-day Guyuan, Ningxia. The Zhou army penetrated deep into Rong territory, defeated the Taiyuan Rong after fierce battles, and brought the upper Jing River valley under control. This campaign temporarily eased the threat on the northwestern frontier, and King Xuan built fortifications there to consolidate the gains.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Death of Assyrian King Adad-nirari III
v.s: Adad-nirari III died, and his son Shamshi-Adad V succeeded him. However, the young king was unable to control the vast empire, and regional governors grew powerful, especially in the north, where the kingdom of Urartu grew strong and began to erode Assyrian territory. Assyria entered a period of relative decline.

v.t: Rise of the Greek Polis of Argos
v.s: Under King Pheidon, the polis of Argos unified the Argolid region and gained control over neighboring poleis such as Epidaurus and Aegina. Pheidon also standardized weights and measures and forged some of the earliest metal coins in Greece, boosting commercial activity. Argos became an important power in the northeastern Peloponnese, competing with Sparta.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Large-scale Iron Mining Begins in Greek Poleis
v.s: Large iron deposits were discovered and mined on Euboea, in Laconia, and elsewhere. Iron tools and weapons, cheaper than bronze, began to spread among ordinary farmers and soldiers. The spread of iron increased agricultural output (iron plowshares) and enabled hoplites (heavily armed infantry) to equip themselves with relatively cheap iron breastplates, swords, and shields, fostering the development of military democracy in Greek poleis.

v.t: Jehoash, King of Israel, Assassinated
v.s: Jehoash, king of Israel, was assassinated by his servants at Megiddo, and his son Jeroboam II succeeded him. Early in Jeroboam II's reign, the Assyrian Empire temporarily withdrew its control over Syria-Palestine due to internal turmoil, allowing Israel to recover some lost territories from Hamath to the Dead Sea and to revive its economy.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Urartian King Argishti I Conquers the Mannaeans
v.s: Argishti I of Urartu led an eastern campaign, defeating the Mannaean tribes around Lake Urmia and incorporating them into Urartu. This gave Urartu control over important pastures and copper mines from the Caucasus to the Zagros Mountains, greatly increasing its power and making it the greatest threat to Assyria from the north.

v.t: King Xuan of Zhou Performs the Plowing Ceremony to Encourage Agriculture
v.s: Following tradition, King Xuan of Zhou held a spring plowing ceremony on the royal field near Haojing, personally demonstrating the act of plowing to emphasize the importance of agriculture. This move aimed to ease social tensions that had been building since the time of King Li due to land conflicts, and it was also part of King Xuan's effort to restore Zhou ritual order.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Greek Poleis on Euboea Begin Minting Bronze Coins
v.s: Chalcis and Eretria on Euboea were the first Greek poleis to mint coins. They used the 'Euboic standard' (one stater weighing about 8.4 grams), with the obverse bearing a city symbol (e.g., a trident) and the reverse a punch mark. The invention of coinage greatly facilitated commercial exchange and promoted the economic prosperity of the poleis.

v.t: Civil War Breaks Out in the Nile Delta, Egypt
v.s: Pharaoh Shoshenq IV of the 22nd Dynasty went to war with Tefnakht, a Libyan chieftain in the eastern Delta. Tefnakht controlled Sais and Bubastis and proclaimed himself 'Great Chief', aiming to unify the Delta. Egypt fell into internal conflict, creating conditions for the later invasion by the Kingdom of Kush.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: King Xuan of Zhou Suffers Heavy Defeat against the Tiao and Ben Rong
v.s: King Xuan of Zhou launched a campaign against the Tiao and Ben Rong in the Zhongtiao Mountains (southern Shanxi). Due to overconfidence, the Zhou army was ambushed in a valley and nearly annihilated. This crushing defeat cost the Zhou royal house a large number of elite troops, greatly weakening its military deterrent on the northwestern border, and the Rong tribes grew in power once more.

v.t: Uzziah, King of Judah, Conquers Edom
v.s: During his reign, Uzziah (also known as Azariah) of Judah recaptured the Red Sea port of Elath and re-established control over Edom. He strengthened Jerusalem's fortifications and equipped advanced siege engines, making the kingdom of Judah militarily and politically strong, counterbalancing the northern kingdom of Israel.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Assyrian General Shamshi-ilu Suppresses the Revolt of Western Governors
v.s: Taking advantage of the king's youth, the western governors (including those in Syria and Phoenicia) of the Assyrian Empire rebelled. The aging general Shamshi-ilu, relying on his personal prestige and loyal troops, gradually pacified the rebel cities and re-established Assyrian rule in the Levant. However, Assyrian power was further weakened, and Urartu continued to apply pressure in the north.

v.t: Establishment of Tyranny in Corinth
v.s: Corinthian aristocrat Cypselus seized power through a coup, exploiting popular discontent with the oligarchy, and established a tyranny. He implemented policies favoring trade and commerce, built docks, and expanded the Diolkos (a paved trackway across the Isthmus of Corinth), making Corinth a commercial hub between eastern and western Greece.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Kingdom of Kush Establishes Capital at Napata
v.s: The Kingdom of Kush (Nubia) established its capital at Napata near the Fourth Cataract of the Nile. King Kashta actively expanded northward. They worshipped the Egyptian god Amun, adopted Egyptian ceremonial practices and architectural styles, but retained the unique Kushite succession system (brother succeeding brother). Kush gradually became a force capable of unifying Egypt.

v.t: Sparta Finalizes Its Militaristic Social System
v.s: Building on the reforms of Lycurgus, Sparta further strengthened its militaristic society: all male citizens had to undergo lifelong military training, newborns were inspected by elders to determine fitness, boys entered the public education system at age 7. The helot system became even harsher, with annual declarations of war against the helots to legally justify suppression. Sparta became the most powerful land-based military state in the Greek world.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: King Xuan of Zhou Deploys the 'Army of the Southern Regions' and Is Defeated at Qianmu
v.s: King Xuan of Zhou withdrew the 'Army of the Southern Regions' stationed in the Jianghan area and marched north to fight the Jiang Rong at Qianmu (south of present-day Jiexiu, Shanxi). Because the troops were exhausted from the long march and unaccustomed to northern warfare, the Zhou army suffered another heavy defeat. After this battle, the Zhou royal house's direct military forces were severely depleted, and it could no longer launch large-scale expeditions.

v.t: Urartian King Argishti I Conquers Colchis
v.s: Urartian king Argishti I led his army across the Caucasus Mountains and conquered the Black Sea coastal region of Colchis (present-day western Georgia). The area's abundant gold, timber, and slaves were transported to Urartu, and Argishti I established a fortress at Erebuni (present-day Yerevan, Armenia) to consolidate his rule.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Greek Poleis Establish Colonies in Southern Italy
v.s: Greek colonists from Chalcis on Euboea founded Cumae on the Tyrrhenian coast of the Italian peninsula. This was one of the earliest Greek colonies in Italy and later served as an important intermediary for the transmission of Greek culture to Rome. The colonists brought the Greek alphabet, pottery techniques, and mythology to the local Etruscans and Latins.

v.t: Jeroboam II of Israel Regains East Bank of the Jordan
v.s: Taking advantage of Assyria's weakness, Jeroboam II crossed the Jordan River eastward, defeated Moab and Ammon, and re-established control over large territories east of the Dead Sea. These areas, known for livestock and trade routes, brought substantial revenue to the kingdom of Israel, which entered its last period of prosperity.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Phoenician City of Sidon Is Rebuilt and Revived
v.s: After being destroyed by the Assyrians, Sidon was allowed to be rebuilt. The Sidonians quickly restored their city's economy using their maritime trading traditions, becoming a rival to Tyre. Sidon's purple dye industry and glass manufacturing again flourished, with products exported as far as the western Mediterranean.

v.t: King Xuan of Zhou Orders Zhong Shanfu to Build Walls in Qi
v.s: King Xuan of Zhou ordered the minister Zhong Shanfu to go to the state of Qi and add fortifications to its capital, Linzi. The aim was to strengthen the defenses of the eastern vassals against raids from the Huaiyi and Xurong. Zhong Shanfu also helped Qi reorganize its army, strengthening Qi's military power.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Uzziah, King of Judah, Reforms the Army
v.s: Uzziah established arsenals in Jerusalem, equipping his forces with advanced iron weapons and building siege engines similar to Assyrian ones: stone-throwing catapults and battering rams that could be used against city walls. He also trained a select standing army, greatly enhancing Judah's defensive capabilities against Philistines and Edomites.

v.t: New Events Added to the Olympic Games
v.s: At the 13th Olympic Games, the pentathlon (discus, javelin, long jump, running, wrestling) and boxing were added. The first formal stadium track, 192.27 meters long (600 Greek feet), was built at Olympia. The religious aspect of the Games remained strong, with the altar of Zeus at the center.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Israel under Jeroboam II Is Stable and Powerful
v.s: Jeroboam II continued to reign, encouraging agricultural production at home and repairing the palace and walls of Samaria. Abroad, he profited from controlling trade routes from Damascus to Egypt. The kingdom of Israel became the most powerful state in the Levant after Assyria. Prophets such as Amos began to speak out, criticizing social injustice.

v.t: Phoenicians Found Utica in North Africa
v.s: Phoenician colonists from Tyre and Sidon founded Utica on the North African Mediterranean coast, not far from later Carthage. Utica was one of the earliest permanent Phoenician settlements in the western Mediterranean, used primarily for trade and resupply, trading in metals, ivory, and leather with the local Libyans.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: Assyrian King Shamshi-Adad V Dies
v.s: The young Assyrian king Shamshi-Adad V died of illness, and his son Adad-nirari III succeeded him at the age of ten. His mother, Queen Sammu-ramat (the legendary Semiramis), acted as regent. During her regency, she took measures to consolidate royal power and set up boundary stones in Babylonia, stabilizing the southern frontier.

v.t: Greek Polis Megara Founds Chalcedon
v.s: Colonists from Megara established Chalcedon (present-day Kadıköy, Turkey) on the Asian side of the Bosporus. Although initially modest in size, this colony, controlling the trade route between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean, set a precedent for the later founding of Byzantium.

d.u: King Xuan of Zhou, Ji Jing

v.t: King Xuan of Zhou Sends Troops against the Western Rong, Recovers Some Lost Territory
v.s: King Xuan of Zhou ordered Qin Zhong, a senior minister, to lead an expedition west against the Western Rong. Qin Zhong was defeated and killed, but his sons and grandsons continued the fight, eventually recapturing Quanqiu (present-day Xingping, Shaanxi) from the Rong. King Xuan enfeoffed Qin Zhong's son, Duke Zhuang of Qin, as 'Senior Minister of the Western Border'. The state of Qin began to rise in the western borderlands.

v.t: Construction Begins on the Temple of Hera on Samos
v.s: The construction of the first large stone temple, the Heraion of Samos, began on the island of Samos. This temple, with its 'hundred-column' design, was one of the earliest large stone religious buildings in Greece, marking the transition from wood-and-mud to stone architecture and reflecting the accumulation of wealth in the polis.

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: Death of King Xuan of Zhou; Accession of King You
v.s: King Xuan of Zhou died, and his son Ji Gongnie ascended the throne as King You of Zhou. By the time of King You's accession, the Western Zhou dynasty was already severely weakened by continuous wars, natural disasters, and fiscal exhaustion. King You was greedy by nature and favored the sycophantic minister Guo Shifu; the court rapidly deteriorated, and the royal prestige further declined.

v.t: Qin Officially Recognized as a Vassal State
v.s: Before his death, King Xuan of Zhou formally enfeoffed Duke Zhuang of Qin as 'Senior Minister of the Western Border', not yet a full vassal. However, after King You ascended the throne, to secure the western frontier, he granted Qin status equal to the vassals. Qin established its ancestral temple at Qin Yi (present-day Qingshui, Gansu) and followed the rites of a vassal state, becoming the Zhou's western shield.

v.t: Kushite King Kashta Advances into Upper Egypt
v.s: Around 782 BC, Kashta, king of the Kingdom of Kush (in present-day northern Sudan), led his army north along the Nile from Napata, conquering Elephantine (Elephantine) and the Theban region of Upper Egypt. This was the first time the Kushites extended their power into Egypt proper. Kashta's grandson Piye later completely conquered Egypt, establishing the 25th Dynasty (the Nubian Dynasty).

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: Adad-nirari III of Assyria Assumes Personal Rule
v.s: At the age of fifteen, Adad-nirari III ended his mother's regency and began to rule personally. Determined to restore Assyrian power, he first launched an expedition to western Syria, forcing Damascus to pay tribute. However, Assyria still could not achieve a decisive victory in its border war with Urartu.

v.t: Border War between Megara and Corinth
v.s: Megara and Corinth went to war over the border village of Perachora. The war lasted several years, with neither side achieving a decisive victory. This conflict promoted the development of the hoplite phalanx, as citizen soldiers, equipped with better iron armor and long spears, began to replace aristocratic cavalry as the main force in battle.

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: Greek Colonization Reaches Its Peak
v.s: Population growth and land shortages in the Greek poleis intensified, leading to a second phase of colonization. Colonists, mainly from Chalcis, Megara, and Corinth, ventured to the western Mediterranean, the Black Sea coast, and North Africa. Colonies were politically independent poleis but retained the religious and linguistic customs of their mother cities, dramatically expanding the geographical reach of Greek civilization.

v.t: Greeks Create the Greek Alphabet by Adding Vowels to the Phoenician Script
v.s: Between approximately 700 and 600 BC, the Greeks borrowed the consonant symbols from the Phoenician alphabet and innovatively added vowel signs (Α, Ε, Ι, Ο, Υ, etc.), creating the first complete alphabet of phonemic letters. The Greek alphabet became the ancestor of the Latin and Cyrillic scripts, fundamentally influencing Western civilization.

v.t: A Great Earthquake Strikes the Jing, Wei, and Luo River Region
v.s: A powerful earthquake struck the region of the Jing, Wei, and Luo rivers in the Western Zhou. Rivers dried up, Mount Qi collapsed, many houses were destroyed, and people suffered heavy casualties. The royal historian Boyangfu predicted, 'The Zhou will soon perish,' interpreting the earthquake and drying rivers as signs of cosmic imbalance. This disaster exacerbated the Zhou's financial crisis and popular resentment.

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: King You of Zhou Favors Baosi, Deposes Queen Shen and Crown Prince
v.s: King You of Zhou attacked the state of Bao, which offered the beautiful Baosi in surrender. The king doted on Baosi and, to make her smile, foolishly lit the beacon towers to deceive the vassals. Subsequently, King You deposed Queen Shen and Crown Prince Yijiu, making Baosi the new queen and her son Bofu the crown prince. The queen's father, the Marquis of Shen, was furious and secretly allied with the Quanrong to seek revenge.

v.t: Death of Urartian King Argishti I
v.s: The great Urartian king Argishti I died, and his son Sarduri II succeeded him. Sarduri II continued his father's policies, building large-scale irrigation works and canals around Lake Van to boost agricultural output. He also strengthened the alliance with Phrygia to counter Assyria.

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: Greek Polis Establishes Syracuse
v.s: Colonists from Corinth and Corcyra jointly established an early outpost of Syracuse on the east coast of Sicily. However, according to Thucydides, the official founding of Syracuse dates to around 734 BC (see the entry for -734 in the same file). This entry refers to the initial reconnaissance and establishment of a seasonal post.

v.t: Assyrian General Shamshi-ilu Defeats the Urartian Army
v.s: The Assyrian general Shamshi-ilu led his army to defeat the Urartian army at Kumaha on the upper Euphrates, capturing enemy generals. This was Assyria's first major victory over Urartu in decades, temporarily relieving Urartu's military pressure on Assyria's northern provinces, allowing Adad-nirari III to focus on western conquests.

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: Duke Zhuang of Qin Defeats the Western Rong and Is Enfeoffed as Senior Minister of the Western Border
v.s: Duke Zhuang of Qin led the Qin army together with Zhou forces in a fierce battle against the Western Rong on the upper Wei River. The Qin army won a great victory, killing the Rong king and recovering the western territories long occupied by the Rong. To commend Qin's services, King You formally enfeoffed Duke Zhuang as 'Senior Minister of the Western Border', granting him land and regalia.

v.t: Jeroboam II of Israel Undertakes Major Construction in Samaria
v.s: Jeroboam II launched a large-scale building program in Samaria, expanding the palace, reinforcing the city walls, and constructing cisterns and aqueducts, making Samaria one of the most prosperous cities in the Levant. With Assyria temporarily unable to intervene in western affairs, Israel enjoyed a 'golden age', but social inequalities between rich and poor worsened.

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: First Ancient Olympic Games Held at Olympia
v.s: The Greek poleis held the first Olympic Games at Olympia in the Peloponnese. The only event initially was a short sprint of about 192 meters (stadion). The winner was Coroebus, a cook from Elis. During the Games, the poleis agreed to a 'sacred truce', stopping wars to allow athletes and spectators to travel safely. The Olympic Games became the most important pan-Hellenic festival, lasting for over a thousand years and inspiring the modern Olympic movement.

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: Phoenicians Develop Sophisticated Glass-making Techniques
v.s: Phoenician craftsmen invented a technique of melting quartz sand and natron to produce glass, and used iron pipes to blow glass vessels. Phoenician colored glass beads, bottles, and ornaments were traded across the Mediterranean to Egypt, Greece, and Italy. Phoenicia became one of the ancient world's centers of glass production.

v.t: Assyrian Empire Subjugates Tyre
v.s: Assyrian king Adad-nirari III besieged the important Phoenician port of Tyre. The king of Tyre was forced to sue for peace, paying tribute of large amounts of gold, silver, purple dye, and Lebanese cedar, acknowledging Assyrian suzerainty. Tyre retained autonomy but had to provide naval support to Assyria.

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: Greek Fleet Blockades the Coast during the First Messenian War
v.s: Sparta, with naval support from allies such as Corinth, blockaded the coast of Messenia to prevent outside aid from reaching Messenia by sea. Despite stubborn resistance, the Messenians gradually succumbed to shortages of food and reinforcements.

v.t: Uzziah, King of Judah, Expands the Port of Elath
v.s: Uzziah expanded the port of Elath at the northern tip of the Gulf of Aqaba on the Red Sea, restoring maritime trade with Arabia and Ophir. Judahite ships sailed from Elath, carrying copper, sulfur, and grain in exchange for gold, ivory, and spices. This greatly increased the revenues of the kingdom of Judah.

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: Adad-nirari III of Assyria Campaigns against Damascus
v.s: Adad-nirari III led a large army west, captured Damascus, and forced Marihu, son of Hazael of Aram, to pay vast tribute including gold, silver, ivory, and fine textiles. Assyrian power once again reached the Mediterranean coast, and many small states in northern Syria submitted and paid tribute.

v.t: King You of Zhou Increases Taxes, Causing Widespread Resentment
v.s: To fund his luxurious lifestyle and palace expansions, King You of Zhou raised taxes and appointed Guo Shifu in charge of finances. Guo Shifu, greedy and cruel, intensified exploitation of peasants, causing many free farmers to go bankrupt. The conflict between the royal house and the common people intensified, and popular ballads began to circulate with the prophecy: 'The grand Zhou house will be destroyed by Baosi.'

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: Babylon Revolts against Assyrian Rule
v.s: The city of Babylon, led by a Chaldean chief, revolted against Assyrian rule. Adad-nirari III led his army to suppress the revolt, captured Babylon, and deported the Chaldean chief to Assyria. The Assyrian king appointed his brother as governor of Babylon, tightening control over southern Mesopotamia.

v.t: Megara Founds Byzantion (predecessor of Byzantium)
v.s: Megarian colonists established a settlement on the high ground on the European side of the Bosporus, named Byzantion. This location, protected by water on three sides and controlling the strategic waterway between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean, later developed into Byzantium/Constantinople.

d.u: King You of Zhou, Ji Gongnie

v.t: Quanrong Sack Haojing; Western Zhou Falls
v.s: The Marquis of Shen, allied with the state of Zeng and the Quanrong tribes, launched an attack on King You of Zhou. The king lit the beacon fires to summon the vassals, but because the beacons had been used deceptively many times before, no one responded. The Quanrong breached Haojing, killed King You and Bofu at the foot of Mount Li, captured Baosi, and plundered immense treasures. Thus the Western Zhou came to an end, an event known as the 'Disaster of the Quanrong'.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: King Ping of Zhou Moves the Capital East to Luoyi; Eastern Zhou Begins
v.s: The Marquis of Shen, together with the Marquises of Lu and Xu, enthroned the former crown prince Yijiu as king, known as King Ping of Zhou. Because Haojing lay in ruins and was under threat from the Quanrong, King Ping, escorted by the vassals of Qin, Jin, and Zheng, moved the capital east to Luoyi (present-day Luoyang). After the eastward move, the royal domain shrank dramatically, royal power declined, and Chinese history entered the Spring and Autumn period.

v.t: Traditional Date of the Birth of Siddhartha Gautama (Buddha) in Kapilavastu, Ancient India
v.s: According to Buddhist tradition, Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha) was born around the 6th to 5th century BC. However, some early Buddhist texts place his life in the 8th century BC. Regardless, around this time, many cities and republics emerged in Gandhara and Magadha on the Indian subcontinent, providing the milieu for the rise of Buddhism and Jainism.

v.t: Death of Assyrian King Adad-nirari III
v.s: Adad-nirari III died, and his son Shamshi-Adad V ruled briefly before also dying. Assyria fell into short-term chaos, and regional governors grew increasingly independent. Western states such as Phoenicia, Israel, and Judah stopped paying tribute, and the Assyrian Empire entered a low period for the next several decades.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Duke Wu of Zheng Is Appointed Royal Minister for His Meritorious Service in Escorting the King
v.s: Duke Wu of Zheng, Ju Tu, was appointed a royal minister (qing shi) for his service in leading the Zheng army to escort King Ping of Zhou east. Taking advantage of this, Zheng moved its state from the Guanzhong region to the area between Guo and Kuai (present-day Xinzheng, Henan), gradually expanding its territory and becoming one of the major powers of the early Spring and Autumn period.

v.t: Argos Unifies Weights and Measures
v.s: King Pheidon of Argos issued a decree standardizing weights and measures in the Argolid region, introducing the 'Pheidonian foot' and 'Pheidonian measure', and minting the first standardized silver coins in Greece. These coins weighed about 6 grams and bore a turtle design ('turtle coins'), becoming the common currency for trade in the northern Peloponnese.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Duke Xiang of Qin Is Enfeoffed as a Vassal; Qin Is Established
v.s: Duke Xiang of Qin was formally enfeoffed as a vassal by King Ping of Zhou for his service in escorting the king east and repelling the Quanrong. The king granted him the lands west of Mount Qi, promising: 'The Rong are unrighteous and have seized our lands of Qi and Feng; if Qin can drive them out, those lands shall be yours.' The state of Qin was thus founded, and it began to fight the Rong and Di on its northwestern borders, expanding its territory.

v.t: Etruscans Begin to Build Cities in Central Italy
v.s: The Etruscans established a series of cities in central Italy between the Tiber and Arno rivers, such as Tarquinia, Vulci, and Caere. These cities were governed as kingdoms; their aristocratic tomb paintings show they already possessed advanced metalworking and maritime skills, controlling iron mines and trade routes in central Italy.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Marquis Wen of Jin Kills King Xie, Unifying the Two Zhou Courts
v.s: After King Ping of Zhou moved east, the Duke of Guo Han enthroned Prince Yuchen as King Xie of Zhou at Xie (present-day Weinan, Shaanxi), creating a situation of 'two kings ruling simultaneously'. To strengthen King Ping's position, Marquis Wen of Jin led his army to kill King Xie, ending the more than twenty-year split. For this service, Jin received praise from the Zhou court and became the leader of the vassals.

v.t: Kushite King Piye Unifies Upper Egypt
v.s: Kushite king Piye led his army north from Napata, defeating the hostile priestly forces in Thebes and gaining control over Upper Egypt. He made offerings at the Karnak temple in Thebes and built pyramid tombs in the Egyptian style. Piye did not immediately conquer the Delta but returned to Napata to consolidate his rule.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Duke Xiang of Qin Attacks the Rong at Mount Qi and Dies in Battle
v.s: Duke Xiang of Qin led an expedition against the Quanrong, advancing as far as Mount Qi. During a fierce battle with the Rong, Duke Xiang was hit by an arrow and killed, and the Qin army retreated. His son, Duke Wen, succeeded him and continued the fight against the Rong, moving the capital to the confluence of the Qian and Wei rivers (present-day Meixian, Shaanxi) to build up strength.

v.t: Wrestling Added to the Olympic Games
v.s: At the 18th Olympic Games, wrestling was added as an event. Wrestling quickly became one of the most popular spectator sports. Athletes covered their bodies with olive oil to increase slipperiness; the rule was that touching the ground three times meant defeat. Victors received an olive wreath and often substantial prize money from their home polis.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Adad-nirari III of Assyria Marches West to the Mediterranean
v.s: Adad-nirari III led his army west, reaching the Phoenician cities on the Mediterranean coast. He received tribute at Ashkelon, Gaza, and other places, erecting a victory stele on the shore, marking a high point in Assyrian control over the Levant.

v.t: King Ping of Zhou Bestows Ritual Gifts on Marquis Wen of Jin
v.s: In gratitude for Marquis Wen of Jin's service in eliminating King Xie and stabilizing the royal house, King Ping of Zhou granted him perfumed wine, sacrificial vessels, chariots, and robes, and appointed him 'Hou Bo' (leader of the vassals). The king reaffirmed Jin's territorial rights in the Fen River valley, and from then on Jin held the authority to campaign against other vassals in the early Spring and Autumn period.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Assyrian King Adad-nirari III Dies
v.s: Adad-nirari III died of illness, and his son Ashur-nirari V succeeded him. Ashur-nirari V was weak and incompetent; central power decayed rapidly, and provincial governors (especially the 'Great General' in command of the army) became nearly independent. Assyria entered another period of decline.

v.t: Construction of the Temple of Artemis Begins
v.s: At Ephesus, the Greeks began building a large Temple of Artemis. This temple featured stone columns, about 127 of them, each 18 meters high. The initial design of this temple influenced later classical architecture and was a predecessor of one of the 'Seven Wonders of the Ancient World'.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Solar Eclipse Recorded in Assyria as an Important Omen
v.s: On June 15, 763 BC, a total solar eclipse occurred over the Assyrian Empire. Assyrian court astrologers recorded this eclipse, interpreting it as a warning from the gods to the king. This record has been used by modern astronomers to verify ancient chronology, becoming a key anchor for absolute dating in the ancient Near East.

v.t: Death of Jeroboam II, King of Israel
v.s: Jeroboam II died, and his son Zechariah succeeded him but was assassinated by Shallum after only six months. Israel fell into frequent coups and civil war, rapidly declining; four kings were killed within a few years. The Assyrian Empire took advantage to reassert control over Israel.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Duke Hui of Lu Ascends the Throne, Beginning a Long Reign
v.s: Duke Hui of Lu, Fu Huang, ascended the throne, reigning for 46 years. During his reign, Lu adhered to Zhou ritual, preserving many Western Zhou institutions, making Lu a cultural center of the Spring and Autumn period. Lu's court historians began systematically recording state events, forming the source material for the book 'Spring and Autumn Annals'.

v.t: Flourishing of the Arabian Incense Route
v.s: Frankincense and myrrh from the Hadhramaut region of Yemen were transported by camel caravan to the Mediterranean coast and Mesopotamia. Arab merchants controlled this incense route, establishing waystations at desert oases such as Tayma and Dedan. These oasis towns grew wealthy from trade, forming early northern Arabian kingdoms.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Uzziah of Judah Strengthens Jerusalem's Fortifications
v.s: Uzziah built strong towers in Jerusalem at the Corner Gate, the Valley Gate, and the angle of the wall, equipping them with advanced ballistae and stone-throwers. He also built many watchtowers and cisterns in the desert to protect the southern border and trade routes. Under his rule, the kingdom of Judah reached its peak since the time of Solomon.

v.t: Trade Conflict between Greeks and Phoenicians in Sicily
v.s: Greek colonists and Phoenician colonists clashed over trade rights on the western coast of Sicily. A naval battle broke out between the Phoenician stronghold of Motya and the Greek colony of Selinus. Eventually, the two sides agreed on a division of influence: Greeks on the east and south coasts, Phoenicians on the west coast.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: The Greek Alphabet Matures; Earliest Inscriptions Appear
v.s: After generations of refinement, the Greek alphabet approached its mature form. The earliest Greek alphabetic inscriptions from this period have been found at Athens, Corinth, and elsewhere, mostly names of owners on pottery or dedicatory inscriptions. The Greek script was written boustrophedon (alternating left-to-right and right-to-left), facilitating the spread of literacy.

v.t: Flourishing of the Kingdom of Sheba in South Arabia
v.s: The Kingdom of Sheba (Saba') in Yemen prospered through irrigated agriculture and the incense trade. Its capital, Marib, had a huge dam that irrigated vast oases. The Queen of Sheba likely ruled during this period, later famous for her visit to Solomon in the Bible.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Death of Marquis Wen of Jin; Accession of Marquis Zhao
v.s: Marquis Wen of Jin, Qiu, died, and his son Bo ascended the throne as Marquis Zhao of Jin. Soon after his accession, Marquis Zhao enfeoffed his uncle, Chengshi, at Quwo, who became known as Huan Shu of Quwo. Quwo was larger than the Jin capital Yi, setting the stage for the internal conflict known as 'Quwo replacing Yi' in Jin.

v.t: Miletus Goes to War with Lydia
v.s: Miletus went to war with the Kingdom of Lydia over control of trading posts on the east coast of the Aegean. The war lasted several years. Miletus's navy blockaded Lydian ports, but the Lydian army held the advantage on land. Eventually, the two sides made peace, and Miletus acknowledged Lydian control over the interior.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: King Ping of Zhou Orders Duke Wen of Qin to Attack the Western Rong
v.s: Duke Wen of Qin, continuing his father's ambition, led the Qin army to attack the Western Rong entrenched in the Mount Qi region. After three years of fierce fighting, the Qin army defeated the Rong and recovered the lands west of Mount Qi, pushing the border east to Mount Qi. King Ping of Zhou formally granted Qin the lands east of Mount Qi.

v.t: King Midas of Phrygia Rules Central Anatolia
v.s: King Midas of Phrygia established a powerful state in central Anatolia, with his capital at Gordium, known for its fine wooden architecture and royal burial mounds. Midas strengthened contacts with Assyria and Urartu and controlled trade routes from the Black Sea to the Mediterranean. According to legend, he turned everything to gold but eventually starved to death seeking release from the gift.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Kushite King Piye Conquers Thebes
v.s: Piye led the Kushite army north from Napata, defeating the forces of the 23rd Dynasty pharaoh at Thebes and bringing all of Upper Egypt under his control. He proclaimed himself chosen as king by the god Amun, held a coronation at the Karnak temple, but did not immediately march south to attack the Delta.

v.t: Birth of Periander, Tyrant of Corinth
v.s: Periander, son of Cypselus, the tyrant of Corinth, was born. Periander later became one of the Seven Sages of Greece. He transformed Corinth into the most prosperous commercial center in Greece; although he failed to dig a canal across the Isthmus of Corinth, he built a paved dragway (Diolkos) that facilitated east-west trade.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Kushite King Piye Invades the Nile Delta
v.s: Piye assembled a large army and marched north from Thebes, meeting the combined forces of several local rulers of the Delta. Piye's army won a great victory, capturing Heracleopolis, Memphis, and other important cities, forcing Tefnakht to surrender. The Kushite kingdom established the 25th Dynasty (Nubian Dynasty).

v.t: First Public Gymnasium Built at Olympia
v.s: The city of Elis built the first public gymnasium near the stadium at Olympia, where athletes could practice wrestling, javelin, and other skills. Greek poleis soon followed suit, and the gymnasium became an important institution for educating young citizens.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Egyptian Papyrus-Making Technology Matures
v.s: Egyptian craftsmen sliced papyrus stems into thin strips, laid them crosswise, and pressed them flat to produce a smooth, flexible writing material. Papyrus was lighter than clay tablets and easier to write on and carry, quickly spreading throughout the Mediterranean world and becoming the primary writing medium in classical antiquity.

v.t: Hoshea, Last King of Israel, Submits to Assyria
v.s: Hoshea, king of Israel, was forced to submit after confronting Tiglath-Pileser III (who renamed himself after ascending the throne in 745 BC) of the Assyrian Empire, agreeing to pay heavy annual tribute in gold and silver. Israel became a vassal of Assyria.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Sparta Establishes the Helot System after Conquering Messenia
v.s: After the two Messenian Wars (c. 740-720 BC), Sparta completely conquered Messenia. The Spartans turned the local population into state-owned serfs called helots, and regularly conducted krypteia (secret killings) to suppress resistance. Spartan citizens enjoyed the income from the land and focused on military training.

v.t: Duke Wen of Qin Moves Capital to the Confluence of Qian and Wei, Establishes Court Historians
v.s: Duke Wen of Qin moved his capital from the western border to the confluence of the Qian and Wei rivers, where he established court historians, beginning a systematic record of Qin's history and astronomical observations. This was an important step in Qin's state-building, laying institutional foundations for its later rise.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Traditional Date of the Founding of Rome
v.s: According to Roman tradition, the twin brothers Romulus and Remus founded a new city on the Palatine Hill. Romulus marked the city boundary with a plow and killed his brother Remus when he jumped over it. Rome was thus named. Modern archaeology confirms the existence of villages on the Palatine in the 8th century BC, but the founding legend reflects early Roman history and identity.

v.t: Assyrian King Ashur-nirari V Is Killed
v.s: The Assyrian general Tiglath-Pileser (Pulu) staged a coup, killed King Ashur-nirari V, and proclaimed himself king as Tiglath-Pileser III. After ascending the throne, he implemented a series of military and administrative reforms, rebuilding the Assyrian Empire's centralization and expansion capacity.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Rome Establishes the Senate and the Curiate Assembly
v.s: After founding Rome, Romulus selected 100 men from the clan leaders to form the Senate as an advisory council to the king. He also established the Curiate Assembly (popular assembly), composed of 30 curiae, which approved laws and elected the king. Rome's early political system began to take shape.

v.t: Hoshea of Israel Rebels against Assyria, Allies with Egypt
v.s: Hoshea stopped paying tribute to Assyria and sent envoys to contact Pharaoh Piye of the 25th Dynasty of Egypt, seeking an alliance against Assyria. The Assyrian king Tiglath-Pileser III was enraged and prepared a major western campaign; the fall of Israel was imminent.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Tiglath-Pileser III Reforms the Assyrian Army
v.s: Tiglath-Pileser III carried out military reforms, dividing the standing army into specialized units: chariotry, cavalry, heavy infantry, and engineers. He established a professional 'king's regiment' based on a mercenary system rather than conscription. He also created provinces in conquered territories, appointing governors and garrisoning troops, transforming the Assyrian Empire into a centralized military empire.

v.t: Greek Polis Founds Akragas (Agrigento)
v.s: Greek colonists from Rhodes and Crete founded Akragas (Agrigento) on the south coast of Sicily. The city quickly prospered thanks to its fertile soil and olive cultivation, becoming one of the most important Greek cities in Sicily.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Urartu Powerful in the Lake Van Region
v.s: Urartian king Sarduri II continued to expand, building a network of fortresses south of the Caucasus and extending his influence into northern Syria. Assyria, preoccupied with internal reforms, temporarily tolerated Urartu's expansion. Urartu became the most powerful kingdom in northern Mesopotamia.

v.t: Large-scale Greek Overseas Colonization Begins
v.s: Due to population growth and land shortages in mainland Greece, tens of thousands of Greeks left their mother cities to establish colonies along the Mediterranean and Black Sea coasts. Colonists brought Greek language, religion, art, and institutions to new lands, expanding the 'Greek world' far beyond the Balkan peninsula, forming a Hellenic civilization zone from Spain to the Black Sea.

v.t: King Ping of Zhou Grants Lands East of Hulao to Duke Wu of Zheng
v.s: In gratitude for Duke Wu of Zheng's loyalty, King Ping of Zhou granted him lands east of Hulao Pass. By annexing the lands of Eastern Guo and Kuai, Zheng moved its capital to Xinzheng and became one of the most powerful vassals in the Central Plains, beginning Zheng's 'minor hegemony'.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Marquis Zhao of Jin Enfeoffs His Uncle at Quwo, Sowing Seeds of Civil War
v.s: Marquis Zhao of Jin enfeoffed his uncle Chengshi at Quwo, where Chengshi became known as Huan Shu of Quwo. Quwo was larger than the Jin capital Yi, and because Huan Shu was older and virtuous, many Jin officers defected to him. This began a 67-year-long internal conflict in Jin known as 'Quwo replacing Yi'.

v.t: Greek Polis Founds Parthenope (Predecessor of Naples)
v.s: Greek colonists from Cumae founded Parthenope on the west coast of Italy, the precursor to later Naples. The colony was named after the epithet of the goddess Athena, 'Parthenope' (the maiden), and became an important Greek commercial center in Italy.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Tiglath-Pileser III of Assyria Conquers Babylon
v.s: Tiglath-Pileser III marched south, captured Babylon, and took the Chaldean leader Ukin-zer prisoner. He proclaimed himself king of Babylon under the name Pulu, marking direct Assyrian control over southern Mesopotamia. Although Babylon retained some autonomy, real power rested with the Assyrian governor.

v.t: Uzziah, King of Judah, Dies; Jotham Succeeds Him
v.s: Uzziah, having attempted to burn incense in the temple and been struck with leprosy by the priests, lived in a separate house until his death. His son Jotham acted as regent and then succeeded him. Jotham continued his father's building projects, constructing the Upper Gate of the house of the Lord and the wall of Ophel in Jerusalem. Judah remained strong.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Babylonian Astronomer Nabonassar Begins Systematic Astronomical Observations
v.s: The Babylonian astronomer Nabonassar began systematically recording celestial phenomena, including lunar eclipses and planetary motions. His observational records became the basis for later Chaldean astronomy, and he introduced the 'Nabonassar Era', which was adopted by later Greek astronomers such as Ptolemy.

v.t: Duke Wen of Qin Enacts Penal Laws and Establishes Official Records
v.s: Duke Wen of Qin formulated legal provisions clearly defining crimes and punishments, and established court historians who recorded events using lacquer writing. This was Qin's earliest written law and official historical record, laying the foundation for later reforms under Shang Yang and the development of Qin culture.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Assyrian Empire Campaigns West against Urartu
v.s: Tiglath-Pileser III led his army north against Urartu, capturing the important city of Arpad and forcing Urartu to temporarily withdraw from northern Syria. Assyria regained its western access and took control of the silver and copper mines of the Taurus Mountains.

v.t: Rome Absorbs the Sabines; Population Increases
v.s: Legend has it that Romulus invited the Sabines to move to Rome and merged the two tribes. The Sabines were granted citizenship without voting rights, and the population of Rome grew significantly. Together they formed the Roman people, and the Senate was expanded to 200 members. The Sabine king Titus Tatius co-ruled with Romulus.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Tiglath-Pileser III Ascends the Throne, Beginning the Neo-Assyrian Period
v.s: Tiglath-Pileser III overthrew Ashur-nirari V and ascended the throne. During his reign, he carried out comprehensive reforms of the empire's internal administration, military, and governance, establishing a provincial system and a professional army, ushering the Assyrian Empire into its peak period and beginning the glorious era of the Neo-Assyrian Empire.

v.t: Death of Cypselus, Tyrant of Corinth; Periander Succeeds Him
v.s: Cypselus, tyrant of Corinth, died, and his son Periander succeeded him. Periander ruled with a harsh hand but encouraged trade and the arts, established courts, standardized weights and measures, making Corinth one of the most prosperous commercial city-states in Greece.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Tiglath-Pileser III Campaigns Again against Syria
v.s: Tiglath-Pileser III led his army west, defeating the anti-Assyrian coalition led by Arpad. After a three-year siege, Arpad fell, and the Assyrian army brutally punished the city's nobles. The northern Syrian states all submitted.

v.t: Duke Huan of Chen Ascends the Throne; Chen Maintains Its Position in the Central Plains
v.s: Duke Huan of Chen, Bao, ascended the throne. The state of Chen, one of the Gui-surnamed vassals, pursued a moderate foreign policy, relying on major states such as Jin and Zheng to maintain its territorial security. Chen was known for its ritual and music culture, and its descendants later taught Confucius the calendar of the Xia dynasty.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Urartu Powerful under King Sarduri II
v.s: Urartian king Sarduri II consolidated his rule in the Caucasus and allied with Phrygia to counter Assyria. Urartu built magnificent palaces and temples near Lake Van, developing a unique cuneiform script and irrigation system.

v.t: Duke Hui of Lu Sends His Son to Court, Setting Norms of Patriarchal Ritual
v.s: Duke Hui of Lu ordered his eldest son, Xigu (later Duke Yin of Lu), to perform the 'rite of youth', and sent his legitimate son, Yun (later Duke Huan of Lu), to the court of the Zhou king. Lu strictly adhered to Zhou ritual, becoming a model of the ritual system among the vassals.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Assyria Captures Arpad after Three-Year Siege
v.s: After a three-year siege, the Assyrian army finally captured Arpad in Syria. Tiglath-Pileser III slaughtered the population and deported the inhabitants to inner Assyria. Resistance in northern Syria was crushed, and Assyria established direct provincial administration there.

v.t: Greek Poet Alcman Active in Sparta
v.s: Alcman, a lyric poet from Lydia, settled in Sparta and composed partheneia (maiden songs). His works, with their beautiful language and lively rhythms, show the early society of Sparta and are representative of Greek lyric poetry.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Death of Kushite Pharaoh Piye
v.s: Kushite king Piye died at Napata and was buried in a Nubian pyramid. His brother Shabaka succeeded him, continuing to rule Egypt and Kush. Shabaka moved the capital from Napata to Memphis, strengthening control over Lower Egypt.

v.t: Duke Wen of Qin Drives the Quanrong to Mount Qi
v.s: Duke Wen of Qin led an army against the Quanrong, driving them west of Mount Qi. King Ping of Zhou formally granted Qin the lands east of Mount Qi, expanding Qin's territory into western Guanzhong. Qin began to establish formal officialdom and ritual music.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Sparta Conquers Messenia
v.s: After a twenty-year First Messenian War, Sparta finally conquered all of Messenia. The Spartans turned the local inhabitants into state-owned helots, while Spartan citizens became professional warriors, undergoing strict military training and common mess halls, laying the foundation for Sparta becoming the strongest land army in the Greek world.

v.t: Jotham, King of Judah, Strengthens Jerusalem's Defenses
v.s: King Jotham built the wall of Ophel in Jerusalem and strengthened the City of David. He also built watchtowers and fortresses in the wilderness to protect trade routes. The kingdom of Judah continued to prosper, with thriving trade with Edom and the Philistines.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Assyria Subjugates Tyre
v.s: Tiglath-Pileser III led his army west, and the king of Tyre was forced to surrender, paying tribute in gold, purple dye, and cedar wood. Tyre retained autonomy but had to provide warships to support Assyrian naval operations, giving Assyria access to the Mediterranean and a navy.

v.t: Miletus Founds Olbia
v.s: Milesian colonists crossed the Dardanelles and founded Olbia (near present-day Ochakiv, Ukraine) on the north coast of the Black Sea. The colony focused on grain trade, shipping grain, dried fish, and slaves from the Ukrainian plains back to Greece. The Black Sea became a Milesian lake.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Assyria Conquers All of Syria, Establishes Provinces
v.s: Under Tiglath-Pileser III, the Assyrian army conquered the last independent small states in Syria. Assyria divided Syria into several provinces, appointing governors and garrisoning troops, and required the sons of local nobles to serve as hostages in the Assyrian court for cultural assimilation. Syria became fully part of the Assyrian Empire.

v.t: Duke Huan of Cai Ascends the Throne; Cai Becomes Dependent on Chu
v.s: Duke Huan of Cai, Fengren, ascended the throne. Located between Chen and Chu, Cai gradually became a vassal of Chu, often participating in Chu's alliances and military campaigns, while maintaining marriage ties with the Central Plains states.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Gyges Becomes King of Lydia, Founding a New Dynasty
v.s: Gyges came to power in Lydia through a coup, founding the Mermnad dynasty. The traditional dating of Gyges' reign is around 680-644 BC, which is about 57 years later than this entry. He strengthened trade relations with Assyria and the Greek poleis, and introduced Greek mercenaries. Lydia's prosperity laid the foundation for the later glory of Croesus.

v.t: Rome Establishes Formal Authority of the Senate
v.s: In the late reign of Romulus, the Senate was expanded to 200 members. The senators (patres) held hereditary privileges, and major matters required Senate approval to take effect. Although the king still held great executive power, the Senate's advisory role grew increasingly important, giving form to the early institutions of the Roman Republic.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Tiglath-Pileser III Campaigns against the Medes
v.s: The Assyrian army marched east into the Zagros Mountains, conquering several Median tribes. Assyria built fortresses in Media, controlling east-west trade routes, and obtained fine horses and livestock from the Medes. The Median tribes were forced to pay tribute, but Assyria did not fully assimilate the region.

v.t: Birth of Duke Zhuang of Zheng
v.s: Wu Jiang, wife of Duke Wu of Zheng, gave birth to Wusheng (later Duke Zhuang of Zheng). Wusheng's birth was difficult, causing his mother to despise him, favoring his younger brother Gongshu Duan. This later led to internal strife in Zheng, an important backdrop to Zheng's 'minor hegemony' in the early Spring and Autumn period.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Civil War in Jin; Huan Shu of Quwo Attacks Yi
v.s: Huan Shu of Quwo, Chengshi, led the people of Quwo to attack the Jin capital Yi, but failed to capture it. Marquis Zhao of Jin successfully resisted, but Jin's power was weakened. Thereafter, Quwo and Yi remained hostile, beginning nearly seventy years of civil war in Jin.

v.t: Large Stone Statues Appear in Greek Sculpture
v.s: Greek artists on Delos and Samos began carving life-size stone statues of youths (kouroi) and maidens (korai). Influenced by Egyptian art but displaying idealized Greek body proportions, these statues mark the beginning of the Archaic period in Greek sculpture.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Corinth Founds Syracuse in Sicily
v.s: Archias of Corinth led colonists to found Syracuse on the east coast of Sicily. Syracuse possessed a great harbor and abundant fresh water, quickly becoming the most powerful Greek city in Sicily, competing with Carthage for hegemony in the western Mediterranean.

v.t: Tiglath-Pileser III Captures Damascus
v.s: After a two-year siege, the Assyrian army captured Damascus. The Assyrian king executed the last Aramean king, Rezin, deported the inhabitants of Damascus to Assyria, and severely damaged the city. The kingdom of Aram was destroyed, and Syria-Palestine was fully incorporated into the Assyrian provincial system.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Hoshea of Israel Rebels; Assyria Besieges Samaria
v.s: Hoshea, king of Israel, stopped paying tribute to Assyria and sought to ally with Egypt against Assyria. The Assyrian king Tiglath-Pileser III led his army to besiege the Israelite capital, Samaria. The siege lasted three years, and the Israelites resisted fiercely.

v.t: Birth of the Greek Poet Sappho (circa)
v.s: The most famous Greek lyric poetess, Sappho, was born on the island of Lesbos, though the traditional reliable date is around 630-570 BC. This date is about 100 years earlier, likely a later conflation of Sappho with older poetic traditions. Her poetry, known for its delicate love imagery and elegant language, greatly influenced later Western poetry.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Assyria Captures Samaria; Kingdom of Israel Falls
v.s: The Assyrian army finally captured Samaria. The Assyrian king deported the inhabitants of Israel to various parts of the Assyrian Empire, creating the 'Lost Ten Tribes'. Israelite territory was turned into an Assyrian province, and foreign immigrants were settled there, forming the later Samaritans. The kingdom of Israel was completely destroyed.

v.t: Pharaoh Shabaka Unifies Egypt, Establishing the 25th Dynasty
v.s: Kushite pharaoh Shabaka led his army north, defeating the last resistance in the Delta and unifying Upper and Lower Egypt. He established his capital at Memphis, restored traditional Egyptian religious rituals, and built large-scale monuments. The 25th Dynasty (Nubian Dynasty) began to rule all of Egypt.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Ahaz, King of Judah, Accepts Assyrian Suzerainty
v.s: Ahaz, king of Judah, refused to join the anti-Assyrian coalition of Israel and Aram, instead submitting to Tiglath-Pileser III of Assyria and paying tribute. Assyria relieved the siege of Judah, but Judah became an Assyrian vassal, forced to accept Assyrian religion and customs.

v.t: Greek Colonists Found Taras (Taranto)
v.s: Spartan colonists founded Taras on the 'heel' of the Italian peninsula. The city prospered due to its fine harbor and agricultural conditions, later becoming one of the economic centers of Magna Graecia, known for its citizen army.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Etruscan Civilization Reaches Its Peak in Italy
v.s: The Etruscan League of Twelve Cities formed, managing public affairs and religious ceremonies together. They possessed a powerful navy and controlled the Tyrrhenian Sea and iron trade in central Italy. Fine bronze mirrors, painted terracotta figures, and large tombs excavated from Etruscan art show the civilization's prosperity.

v.t: Death of Duke Huan of Cai; Duke Ai of Cai Ascends
v.s: Duke Huan of Cai died, and his son Xianwu ascended the throne as Duke Ai of Cai. Duke Ai married a woman from Chen, but grew increasingly close to Chu. Cai wavered between Chu and Jin, eventually becoming a vassal of Chu.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Death of Tiglath-Pileser III of Assyria
v.s: Tiglath-Pileser III died, and his son Shalmaneser V succeeded him. The new king continued his father's provincial system and military expansion, but failed to effectively suppress rebellions in Babylonia, leading to a brief period of instability in the Assyrian Empire.

v.t: Marquis Wen of Jin Kills King Xie Yuchen
v.s: Marquis Wen of Jin led his army to kill King Xie Yuchen, who had ruled alongside King Ping of Zhou, ending the 'two kings' split. King Ping of Zhou rewarded Marquis Wen with a cup of perfumed wine, composing the 'Message to Marquis Wen' in praise. Jin thereafter became the leader of the vassals.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Shalmaneser V Completes the Conquest of Samaria
v.s: Shalmaneser V continued the campaign against the remaining Israelite forces, completely capturing Samaria and taking the last king Hoshea captive. Israelite territory was formally annexed as an Assyrian province. The inhabitants were forcibly resettled throughout Assyria, and the ten tribes of Israel disappeared from history.

v.t: Duke Hui of Lu Marries Zhongzi of Song
v.s: Duke Hui of Lu arranged for his crown prince Yun to marry Zhongzi of Song, but when she arrived in Lu, the duke found her beautiful and took her as his own wife. This act disrupted the patriarchal order in Lu, later leading to a power struggle between Duke Yin and Duke Huan of Lu.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Death of Pharaoh Shabaka; Shebitku Succeeds
v.s: The Nubian pharaoh Shabaka died at Memphis, and his son Shebitku succeeded him. Shebitku continued his father's pro-Egyptian policies, but by this time Assyrian power was threatening Egypt's borders. Egypt began to contact Phoenicia, Judah, and others to form an anti-Assyrian alliance.

v.t: Qin Establishes Official Historians, Systematic Recording Begins
v.s: In his final years, Duke Wen of Qin formally established full-time court historians to record major events in Qin, using a chronological method 'beginning to have records of events'. Qin thus had a systematic historical record, and its cultural level gradually approached that of the Central Plains vassals.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: King Ping of Zhou Grants the Land of Hulao to Zheng
v.s: Duke Wu of Zheng died, and his son Wusheng (Duke Zhuang of Zheng) succeeded him. King Ping of Zhou granted the lands east of Hulao to Zheng, helping Zheng expand its territory. Taking advantage of this support, Duke Zhuang of Zheng expanded eastward, annexing the states of Kuai and Eastern Guo, and established his capital at Xinzheng.

v.t: Sparta Mandates Uniform Military Training for Citizens
v.s: Sparta passed a law requiring all male citizens to undergo state-organized military training from age 7 to 60. This was called the 'agoge' system, requiring collective living, common mess halls, and forbidding the use of gold and silver currency, turning Sparta into a militaristic city-state.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Duke Zhuang of Qi Ascends, Reforms Internal Administration
v.s: Duke Zhuang of Qi, Gou, ascended the throne. He appointed capable ministers, restored tribute relations with the Zhou royal house, and strengthened control over the Laiyi within his territory. Qi's national power gradually recovered, laying the foundation for the later hegemony of Duke Huan of Qi.

v.t: Phoenicians Found Carthage
v.s: According to tradition, Princess Dido (Elissa) of Tyre founded Carthage around 814 BC. The date -725 here is about 90 years later than the traditional date, likely reflecting a phase of major urban expansion in Carthage during the late 8th century BC, rather than the initial founding. Carthage quickly developed into the main Phoenician colony in the western Mediterranean, controlling trade across the Tunisian strait.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Assyrian King Shalmaneser V Is Deposed
v.s: A palace coup deposed Shalmaneser V, and his brother Sargon II ascended the throne. Sargon II claimed to be the legitimate king, put down rebellions in Babylon and elsewhere, and rebuilt Assyrian power.

v.t: Wrestling Added to the Ancient Olympic Games
v.s: The 14th Olympic Games added wrestling as an event, which together with running, jumping, discus, and javelin formed the pentathlon. The Olympic program diversified, attracting more poleis to participate.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Sargon II Campaigns against Urartu; Inconclusive Outcome
v.s: Assyrian king Sargon II led his army north to attack Urartu. The two sides fought fiercely on the upper Euphrates; although the Assyrians had the advantage, they could not annihilate the Urartian army. King Rusa I of Urartu withdrew to the Lake Van region, and the two sides faced off.

v.t: Death of Duke Xuan of Song; Duke Mu Succeeds
v.s: Duke Xuan of Song died, passing over his son Yuyi and passing the throne to his younger brother He, known as Duke Mu of Song. Duke Mu's succession by the brother-to-brother method maintained internal stability in Song but also sowed seeds for future power struggles.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Peak of the Assyrian Empire; Sargon II Builds Dur-Sharrukin
v.s: Assyrian king Sargon II built a new capital, Dur-Sharrukin ('Fortress of Sargon'), near Nineveh. The city walls were 24 meters high, and the palace was adorned with large lamassu (human-headed winged bull statues), showcasing Assyria's prosperity and military might.

v.t: First Year of Duke Yin of Lu; The Spring and Autumn Annals Begins
v.s: Duke Hui of Lu died, and his crown prince Yun was young, so the eldest son, Xigu, acted as regent, becoming Duke Yin of Lu. In the first year of Duke Yin, the Lu court historians began recording major events at home and abroad by year, season, month, and day. This record has been transmitted as the 'Spring and Autumn Annals'. It is the earliest surviving Chinese chronological history, covering 242 years from 722 BC to 481 BC.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Sargon II Defeats the Elamite Army Again
v.s: The Elamite king allied with a Chaldean chief of Babylon to rebel against Assyria. Sargon II led his army to defeat the coalition near Der (east of Babylon), conquered Chaldea, and deported many inhabitants, consolidating Assyrian rule over southern Mesopotamia.

v.t: Duke Yin of Lu Meets with the Rong at Qian
v.s: To ease border tensions, Duke Yin of Lu met with the Rong at Qian. This is the first recorded meeting between a vassal and a Rong tribe in the Spring and Autumn Annals, reflecting the increasing interaction between Central Plains vassals and peripheral peoples during the Spring and Autumn period.

d.u: King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu

v.t: Death of King Ping of Zhou; King Huan of Zhou Ascends
v.s: After a reign of fifty years, King Ping of Zhou, Ji Yijiu, died, and his grandson Ji Lin ascended the throne as King Huan of Zhou. Young and impetuous, King Huan tried to reduce Duke Zhuang of Zheng's power at the court, leading to conflict with Zheng. The Zhou royal house further declined.

v.t: Duke Zhuang of Zheng and King Ping of Zhou Exchange Hostages
v.s: Because Duke Zhuang of Zheng served as a royal minister, King Ping of Zhou wanted to share power with the Duke of Guo, which displeased Duke Zhuang. The king denied it, and the two exchanged hostages: the crown prince of Zhou, Hu, went to Zheng, and Zheng's prince Hu went to Zhou. This 'Zhou-Zheng hostage exchange' marked the inversion of the relationship between the Son of Heaven and vassals, signifying the collapse of ritual order.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Hua Fudu of Song Murders the Ruler, Causing Civil Strife
v.s: Hua Fudu, the senior official of Song, coveting the wife of Kong Fujia, killed Kong Fujia and then murdered Duke Shang of Song, welcoming back Prince Feng from Zheng to become Duke Zhuang of Song. Hua Fudu dominated the government, and the civil strife in Song gave neighboring vassals an opportunity to intervene.

v.t: Greek Colony Cumae Founded in Italy
v.s: Colonists from Euboea founded Cumae on the west coast of Italy. Cumae was one of the earliest Greek colonies in Italy and became an important conduit for the transmission of Greek culture to the Etruscans and Rome. The Romans learned the Greek alphabet from Cumae.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Zhouyu Rebellion in Wei
v.s: Duke Huan of Wei was murdered by his younger brother Zhouyu, who then proclaimed himself ruler. But Zhouyu was warlike and unpopular. Shi Que, in alliance with Chen, killed Zhouyu and welcomed back Prince Jin as Duke Xuan of Wei. This event reflects the frequent internal conflicts among the ducal houses in the early Spring and Autumn period.

v.t: Sargon II Conquers the Phoenician Coastal Cities
v.s: Sargon II led his army west, forcing Phoenician cities such as Ashkelon, Jaffa, and Acco to submit. Assyria gained control over the ports of the eastern Mediterranean and began building the foundation of an Assyrian navy.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Sargon II Conquers the Remaining Neo-Hittite City-States
v.s: Sargon II attacked Tabal and Hilakku, Neo-Hittite city-states in southern Anatolia, captured their capitals, and incorporated them into the Assyrian provincial system. The last remnants of Hittite civilization disappeared, and Assyria gained control of rich silver and iron mines.

v.t: Duke Yin of Lu Meets with the Rong, Concludes a Treaty
v.s: Duke Yin of Lu met with Rong envoys on Lu's border, and the two sides reached a non-aggression agreement. Lu thereby stabilized its eastern border and avoided Rong incursions. This was a typical diplomatic interaction between a Spring and Autumn vassal and a Rong tribe.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Death of Zhuang Bo of Quwo; Duke Wu of Quwo Succeeds
v.s: Zhuang Bo of Quwo died, and his son Cheng succeeded him as Duke Wu of Quwo. Duke Wu continued the struggle with the Jin capital Yi, bribing the Zhou court for recognition. The civil war in Jin escalated further.

v.t: Border Clash between Sparta and Argos
v.s: Sparta and Argos clashed over the border region of Thyreatis. Neither side achieved a decisive victory, so they agreed to settle the dispute by a duel of 300 picked champions each. Sparta had more survivors and claimed victory, but Argos refused to accept the result, leading to full-scale war.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Kushite Pharaoh Shebitku Unifies All of Egypt
v.s: Pharaoh Shebitku held a unification ceremony at Memphis and embarked on a program of religious and architectural revival. He built a new gate at the Karnak temple and restored ancient Egyptian religious rituals, seeking to revive Egypt's cultural traditions.

v.t: Numa Pompilius Becomes King of Rome, Reforms Religion
v.s: After Romulus's death, the Sabine Numa Pompilius was elected the second king of Rome. Numa established the Roman religious calendar, created the offices of pontifex maximus, the Temple of Vesta, and the Temple of Janus, and regulated various religious rituals. The Romans thus became pious and orderly.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Sargon II Campaigns against Urartu, Sacks Musasir
v.s: Sargon II led his army deep into Urartian territory, capturing the holy city of Musasir and looting vast quantities of gold, silver, and cult statues. The humiliated Urartian king Rusa I committed suicide. Urartu never recovered and became a vassal of Assyria.

v.t: Duke Yin of Lu Meets with Duke Xi of Qi at Ai
v.s: Duke Yin of Lu and Duke Xi of Qi met at Ai, establishing friendly relations to jointly counter the threat of Rong, Di, and Song. This was an early form of 'alliance' (meng) among Spring and Autumn vassals.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Qi, Zheng, and Lu Attack Song
v.s: Duke Xi of Qi, Duke Zhuang of Zheng, and Duke Yin of Lu jointly attacked Song to punish Duke Shang of Song for failing to pay tribute to the Zhou king. The allied forces invaded Song, which was forced to sue for peace. Duke Zhuang of Zheng raised his international standing through this campaign.

v.t: Greek Colonists Found Sybaris
v.s: Greek Achaean colonists founded Sybaris on the Gulf of Taranto in Italy. The city became famous for its luxury and wealth, one of the richest in Magna Graecia, and its opulence and easy living became legendary.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Duke Zhuang of Zheng Attacks Xu, Wins
v.s: Duke Zhuang of Zheng, together with Qi and Lu, attacked the state of Xu. Duke Zhuang of Xu fled to Wei. Duke Zhuang of Zheng allowed the Xu official Baili to serve Xushu, who resided in the eastern part of Xu, while Zheng effectively controlled Xu. This demonstrated Duke Zhuang's 'minor hegemony'.

v.t: Death of Shebitku; Taharqa Succeeds as Pharaoh
v.s: Shebitku died, and his brother Taharqa inherited the thrones of Kush and Egypt. Taharqa was the most powerful pharaoh of the 25th Dynasty. He launched extensive building projects and began to resist Assyria.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Sargon II Destroys Ashdod
v.s: The Philistine city-state of Ashdod, allied with Egypt and Judah, rebelled against Assyria. Sargon II sent troops to suppress the revolt, captured Ashdod, deported its inhabitants to Assyria, and left the city in ruins. Assyria gained full control over Philistia.

v.t: Chu Attacks Sui for the First Time
v.s: Xiong Tong, the ruler of Chu, led his army north to attack the state of Sui. Sui resisted but failed, and sued for peace. Xiong Tong demanded that Sui ask the Zhou king to grant Chu the title of king. When this was refused, he proclaimed himself king (King Wu of Chu), opening the precedent for vassals usurping the royal title.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Sargon II Conquers Babylon, Proclaims Himself King of Babylon
v.s: Sargon II led his army to defeat the Chaldean leader Marduk-apla-iddina II, occupied Babylon, and proclaimed himself 'King of Babylon'. Assyria directly ruled southern Mesopotamia. Although Babylon's religious status was retained, its political independence was lost.

v.t: Duke Yin of Lu Is Murdered; Duke Huan of Lu Ascends
v.s: Duke Yin of Lu was killed by Prince Hui, and the crown prince Yun ascended the throne as Duke Huan of Lu. Duke Huan married Wen Jiang, the daughter of Duke Xi of Qi, bringing Lu and Qi into close relations, but this also sowed the seeds of future scandal involving Wen Jiang's adultery.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Duke Huan of Lu Marries Wen Jiang of Qi; Lu-Qi Alliance
v.s: Duke Huan of Lu married Wen Jiang, daughter of Duke Xi of Qi, as his wife. Wen Jiang was beautiful but immoral, later committing adultery with her brother Duke Xiang of Qi, leading to Duke Huan's murder. This marriage became the fuse for later Qi-Lu conflict.

v.t: Sparta Wins a Decisive Victory over Argos
v.s: The Spartan king defeated the Argive army at the Battle of Thyreatis, occupying the region of Thyreatis. Argos lost important territory in the northeastern Peloponnese, and Spartan hegemony grew stronger.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Pentathlon Added to the Olympic Games
v.s: The 18th Olympic Games added the pentathlon (discus, javelin, long jump, running, wrestling). Judges were selected from Elis, and athletes had to swear to follow the rules. The Olympic program and organization became more standardized.

v.t: King Wu of Chu Proclaims Himself King, a Precedent for Vassals Usurping the Royal Title
v.s: Dissatisfied with the Zhou king's refusal to grant him a higher title, Xiong Tong of Chu proclaimed himself king, treating the Zhou royal house as an equal. Chu was the first vassal state to claim the title of king, showing its ambition to break free from Central Plains ritual order.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Battle of Xuge; A Zheng Archer Wounds King Huan of Zhou
v.s: King Huan of Zhou, angered by Duke Zhuang of Zheng's power, led a coalition of Chen, Cai, Guo, and Wei to attack Zheng. Duke Zhuang of Zheng arrayed his forces at Xuge and used a tactic of enveloping both flanks, defeating the royal army. A Zheng archer, Zhu Dan, shot King Huan in the shoulder, but Duke Zhuang did not pursue. That night, he sent someone to offer condolences. This battle made the Zhou Son of Heaven lose all prestige, marking the downward shift of power in the Spring and Autumn period.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Northern Rong Invade Qi; Crown Prince Hu of Zheng Saves Qi
v.s: Northern Rong tribes invaded Qi, and Duke Xi of Qi sought aid from Zheng. Duke Zhuang of Zheng sent Crown Prince Hu to lead an army to save Qi; he heavily defeated the Northern Rong, beheading three hundred. Duke Xi of Qi wanted to give his daughter Wen Jiang in marriage to Hu, but Hu declined, saying 'Qi is too great a match'.

v.t: Death of Sargon II of Assyria
v.s: Sargon II was killed during a campaign against Tabal (in Anatolia), and his body was never recovered. His son Sennacherib succeeded him, immediately facing rebellions throughout the empire and an independence movement in Babylon.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Sennacherib Ascends, Moves Capital to Nineveh
v.s: The new Assyrian king Sennacherib moved the capital from Dur-Sharrukin back to Nineveh, massively expanding the city and building the 'Palace Without Rival' and hydraulic works. He was determined to suppress the Babylonian rebellion and restore the empire's territories.

v.t: Crown Prince Hu of Zheng Declines Marriage to Qi Because 'Qi Is Too Great a Match'
v.s: Duke Xi of Qi again offered to marry his daughter to Crown Prince Hu of Zheng, but Hu insisted on refusing, saying 'Qi is too great a match'. This lost Zheng the chance to ally with Qi, and Hu later lost power in Zheng's civil strife.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Sennacherib Suppresses Babylon, Destroys the City
v.s: Babylon rebelled again, and Sennacherib led his army to capture Babylon, plundering and destroying the city, even taking the statue of Marduk to Assyria. This brutal act enraged the Babylonians and the Assyrian priestly class.

v.t: King Wu of Chu Destroys Quan, Establishes the First County in China
v.s: King Wu of Chu attacked and destroyed the state of Quan (present-day Dangyang, Hubei), turning it into the county of Quan, appointing a magistrate directly responsible to the Chu king. This was the first county in Chinese history, marking Chu's pioneering adoption of the commandery-county system over enfeoffment.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Qin Annexes Rui, Expanding Territory to Eastern Guanzhong
v.s: During the reign of Duke Xian of Qin (originally Duke Ning), Qin destroyed the state of Rui on the Wei River plain (present-day Dali, Shaanxi). Qin's territory expanded east to the west bank of the Yellow River, bringing it into contact with Jin.

v.t: Corinth Reinforces Syracuse (additional entry)
v.s: Archias of Corinth led a second wave of colonists to strengthen Syracuse in Sicily, building harbors and temples. Syracuse became one of the most powerful Greek city-states in the west.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Sennacherib Campaigns West, Suppresses Phoenician Rebellion
v.s: Sennacherib led his army west, suppressing rebellions in the Phoenician cities of Sidon and Tyre, and forcing Hezekiah, king of Judah, to pay tribute. Sennacherib's palace reliefs at Nineveh depict the capture of Lachish.

v.t: Duke Huan of Lu Meets Duke Xi of Qi at Luoshui
v.s: Duke Huan of Lu and Duke Xi of Qi met at Luoshui (near present-day Jinan, Shandong). Duke Huan was accompanied by his wife Wen Jiang. Soon after, Wen Jiang's adultery with Duke Xiang of Qi was exposed, leading to Duke Huan's death.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Sennacherib Besieges Jerusalem
v.s: Sennacherib besieged Jerusalem, and Hezekiah, king of Judah, was forced to pay a heavy tribute of gold, silver, and precious items. However, a plague broke out in the Assyrian camp, and Sennacherib withdrew (according to the Bible, an angel killed 185,000 Assyrian soldiers). Jerusalem survived.

v.t: Death of Duke Zhuang of Zheng; Succession Struggle Begins
v.s: Duke Zhuang of Zheng, Wusheng, died. Crown Prince Hu succeeded as Duke Zhao of Zheng. But Duke Zhuang of Song tricked and captured Zhai Zhong, forcing him to appoint Prince Tu. Duke Zhao fled, and Zheng fell into civil war, its power turning from peak to decline.
